speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five, interview with Regina Weber, on the 10th of March, 1995. This is tape five.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my husband, Bob. And we met 45 years ago, and I think he really has saved my life. He's-- I call him, [NON-ENGLISH], which means a 'saint,' also. Because he has been such an unusually good human being. And of course, I love him. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The feeling is mutual, Regina. I love you, too, very dearly. And we-- our-- at the outset of our marriage, we just knew each other about three months. And I knew I would love her immediately, and that was it. It was just a chemical reaction that took place that really-- that-- that has stayed with me for these many years that we've been married. And-- it just-- I only feel-- I only regret that she had all this terrible, terrible, terrible past. This Holocaust experience has been just awesome. And-- I just recall one time when we were in Binghamton, at Regina's sister's house. And she's-- for the first time, she really let out and told me their experience. And even thinking about it makes me almost choke up a bit. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to say anything to Yolanda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uncontrollable-- uncontrollable crying. It was just--

speaker: interviewee
text: We never said anything to Yolanda.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just-- it was just too awesome for me to encompass how the human condition can be like this. I just-- I still find it difficult to understand how this kind of-- kind of inhumanity can exist in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years after you were married did she tell you this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was a matter of about a year or two.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a year or two. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Want to say something to your grandchildren and your daughter?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say something--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, of course. I mean, this is-- this is the-- this is the dividends of our love for one another. And we have this--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have this wonderful daughter, Yolanda. And she brought on these two gorgeous grandchildren, who I adore and I can hardly wait to see them. [LAUGHS] And we're planning to see them this coming Thursday.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I already have plans for them. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: They're darling. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- these-- this is the positive aspect of our relationship. The unfortunate part about it's her history. And I would love for her never to have had it, but that's-- that's-- that's the way life has been.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I still love you very much, darling, just as much as I--

speaker: interviewer
text: They're going to love it when they get it. They send--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my family's picture. The first one on the left is my sister, [? Chicha. ?] Next to her is Izabella, then my brother, Phillip, my oldest sister, Yolan, and myself, on the right there. And in this second row, on the left you see my younger sister and my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of me in 1943. And I'm 15 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken at my aunt's garden in Munkacs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I forgot to mention it, but this is my sister, who unfortunately couldn't run out of the line. Remember when I was telling you about that? And she was taken to Bergen-Belsen, where she died of typhoid in 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to point her out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she is in between my brother, Phillip, and myself, Regina. This is myself. And this picture was taken in New York, around 1947.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture, I am on the left. Next to me is my fiance, Bob. And sitting on the sofa is my sister, Izabel. And this must have been taken in 1949.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left you'll see my husband, Bob. And right of him is me. And we were on vacation in Canada, in 1953.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my darling daughter, Yolanda. And she looks like she's about two years old there. And to the left of her-- or the right of her, I don't know-- is me-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, oh, oh, this must be 1955.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture, my brother is standing up. His name is Phillip. And the gentlemen with the gray hair, sitting, is David Ben-Gurion. And this picture was taken in 1957.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my darling family, David, my son-in-law, Yolanda, my daughter, and her daughter, their daughter, Hannah. And of course, she got married in-- mm-- the year would be '55, I think. No, no, no. She got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. She was getting what? What was she getting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in this picture my daughter is getting her PhD in clinical psychology at Columbia University.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my darling Hannah, my granddaughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's five years old in this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my grandson, Benjamin. And he is two years old in this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: --he doesn't look like a Jewish kid.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he looks like-- Bob has a brother like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have to unwire you. [INAUDIBLE] the other day. Shut the light. [INAUDIBLE] Let it cool down a minute. Thank you.
